title: BETWEEN HARRISON SPENCER-BURROWES v DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL IN THE MATTER BETWEEN HARRISON SPENCER-BURROWES CLAIMANT AND ROYAL BANK OF CANADA
text:
IN THE JERSEY EMPLOYMENT AND DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL 

 IN THE MATTER BETWEEN 

 HARRISON SPENCER-BURROWES 

 CLAIMANT 

 AND 

 ROYAL BANK OF CANADA 

 RESPONDENT 

 RECONSIDERATION
 JUDGMENT 

 Reference: [2026]
TRE 43 

 Before: Mrs H Westmacott 

 DECISION 

 The claim remains rejected as it
was issued out of time and no extension is granted. 

 REASONS 

 Introduction 

 1. 
 The Claimant has been employed
by the Respondent since 2019 as a Fund Accountant and Finance Officer. 

 2. 
 The Claimant sought to bring a claim against the
Respondent for discrimination. The relevant claim form was presented to the Tribunal
on 4 February 2026. 

 3. 
 The claim form states that the final act of
discrimination occurred on 14 October 2025, when a comment was made by another
employee of the Respondent, which led the Claimant to believe that the reason
he was subsequently unsuccessful in his application for a new role with the
Respondent was discriminatory. 

 4. On 5
February 2026, the Tribunal sent an email to the Claimant rejecting the claim
on the basis that it was presented out of time beause the final act of
discrimination occurred on 14 October 2025, which is more than 8 weeks before
the claim form was presented to the Tribunal on 4 February 2026. 

 5. Later that
day the Claimant sent an email to the Tribunal requesting that the decision to
reject the claim be reconsidered for the following reasons: 

 “ While
I acknowledge that the alleged discriminatory comments occurred on or around 14
October 2025, I did not delay in raising my concerns. I raised the matter
internally at the time and pursued my employer’s formal grievance process in
good faith, believing this to be the appropriate and expected course of action.
That process was lengthy and concluded only recently, following which I
promptly submitted my Claim Form. 

 I was not aware that a tribunal claim needed to be
submitted in parallel with the internal grievance process. At no point did I
deliberately allow the time limit to expire. I acted reasonably and
continuously sought to resolve the matter internally before escalating it
externally. 

 In these circumstances, I respectfully submit that it
would be just and equitable for time to be extended and for the Tribunal to
accept jurisdiction over my claim. I therefore ask that the decision to reject
the claim be reconsidered and, if reconsidered favourably, that the claim be
treated as having been submitted on 4 February 2026. 

 I do not request a hearing for the purposes of this
reconsideration and am content for the application to be determined on the
basis of this written submission.” 

 6. Accordingly,
it is my understanding that the Claimant is requesting a retrospective
extension of the time in which to present his claim, until 4 February 2026. 

 7. Prior to determining
that request, I have first considered the date of the final act of
discrimination. Based on the discrimination alleged in the claim form, in
accordance with article 37(3)(a) of the Discrimination (Jersey) Law 2013 (the “Discrimination
Law”), the date for the final act of discrimination may not have been the date
the comment was made, but the date on which the Claimant was notified that his
application for the new role was unsuccessful. 

 8. The claim
form refers to the Claimant attending an interview for the new role on 30
October 2025 and subsequently being informed his application for the new role
was unsuccessful. According to a grievance outcome letter, which was attached
to the claim form, the Claimant raised a grievance with the Respondent on 25
November 2025 in relation to the comments and subsequent unsuccessful
application. Consequently the Claimant must have been notified that his application
was unsuccessful prior to this date and the final act of discrimination must,
therefore, have occurred sometime before 25 November 2025. 

 9. As this date
is still more than 8 weeks before 4 February 2026, even if this is the correct final
date of discrimination, the claim would still remain rejected, unless an
extension is granted. I have, therefore, gone on to coinsider whether an
extension should be granted in accordance with the Claimant’s request, which
submits an extension should be granted for the following reasons: 

 (1) The Claimant
sought to resolve his grievance reasonably and consistently, without delay, by
promptly raising an internal grievance first, then filing a claim form with the
tribunal the day after the internal grievance was dismissed. 

 (2) The Claimant
did not know it was necessary to submit a tribunal claim in parallel with the
internal grievance. 

 (3) It would be
just and equitable for the time to submit the claim to be extended. 

 Relevant Law 

 10. Articles 37(2)(b)(i)
of the Discrimination Law provides that the tribunal shall not consider a claim
for unfair dismissal unless it is presented to the tribuna l “ before
the end of the period of 8 weeks beginning with the date of the act, or the
last act, to which the complaint relates” . 

 11. Articles 37(2)(b)(ii)
of the Discrimination Law provide the tribunal with a discretion to extend the
8 week time period in which claims can be issued to “ within
such further period as the Tribunal considers reasonable in a case where it is
satisfied that it was not reasonably practicable for the application to be made
before the end of that period of 8 weeks." 

 12. 
 In order to determine whether to grant an extension I
must, therefore, first find that it was “ not reasonably practicable ” to
file the claim within 8 weeks of the last act of discrimination. 

 Reasonably Practicable
Test 

 13. 
 The question of whether it was “ reasonably
practicable ” to file a claim was considered by the deputy chair in the case
of Derouet v Quantum Building Supplies Limited [2024] TRE 22. That case
deals with the late presentation of a claim under the Employment (Jersey) Law
2003 (the “Employment Law”), the relevant provision of which (Article 76(2)(b))
is framed in the same terms as Article 37(2)(b)(ii) of the Discrimination Law. 

 14. 
 In Derouet , the deputy chair accepted the
submissions of the respondent’s representative that “ The burden of proof lies on the claimant in this regard – see Fox v LNF Holdings 104/20 at para 5. ”. 

 15. 
 The deputy chair in Derouet 
then went on to state (at paragraphs 7 and 8) that Skrzat
v Jersey Telecom Group Limited 1055-66/11
remains the leading case in relation to the “ reasonably practicable ”
test, and: 

 “ from
that case (at paragraphs 11 and 12) the following key principles emerge: 

 (1)
The word “practicable” should be interpreted liberally, in favour of the
employee. 

 (2)
However, the time limit of 8 weeks is stated in the [Employment Law] to provide
a framework and certainty to all those affected by the provisions of the [Employment
Law]. 

 (3)
Article 76(2)(a) is to be strictly construed and whilst the Tribunal
is given a discretion to extend that time limit (by Article 76(2)(b)) it will
"only do so in the most exceptional circumstances" for example where
some highly unusual and unforeseen event intervenes in the time between the
[effective date of termination] and filing the form JET1 at the Tribunal
office, or where an applicant genuinely did not know of the time limit or his
rights. 

 (4) However, ignorance of the time limits "would be
difficult to establish now that the [Employment Law] is bedded down in Jersey
life and free, accessible advice is available from JACS and the CAB on
employment law issues". 

 8.
Naturally, each case is to be analysed on its own facts, but the principle
given in Skrzat has been consistently adhered to over the
years since the judgment was given. In support of that assertion, the company
has cited Wharton v Barclays 1406-88/11, Ennis
v Larsen Interiors Limited 14/2013, and O'Leary and GR8
Employment Solutions [2022] TRE 42.” 

 16. The Discrimination Law has now “ bedded down in Jersey life ” for
longer than the Employment Law had at the time of the Skrzat judgment,
and free, accessible advice from JACS and the CAB is also available on
discrimination as well as employment law issues. Specifically, there is guidance on both JACS’ and
the Tribunal’s website regarding deadlines for submitting claims, the latter
stating, under the heading “How soon must I make my claim to the Tribunal?” : 

 “The time limit for making a
claim to the Tribunal depends on the type of claim you are making. Most claims
(Including unfair dismissal and discrimination) should be received by the
Tribunal within 8 weeks from the date when your employment ended or the
incident about which you are complaining happened. It is important that your
claim is presented within the appropriate time limit, if you are unsure you
should take advice on the time limits as soon as possible. 

 Please note that time will start
to run on the date when your employment terminated or when the act of
discrimination occurred… 

 The Tribunal is required to apply
these time limits very strictly.” 

 Impact of Internal Process 

 17. With regard
to whether an ongoing internal process impacts upon it being reasonably
practicable to file claims, there are a number of UK cases that deal with this
question in the context of employment claims. While there is a different test
for extending the time to bring a discrimination claim in the UK, section
111(2)(b) of the UK Employment Rights Act 1996, which relates to the time limit
for filing unfair dismissal claims in the UK, mirrors the wording of Article 37(2)(b)(ii)
of the Jersey Discrimination Law. 

 18. In the case
of Bodha v Hampshire Area Health Authority 1982 ICR 200, the UK
Employment Appeals Tribunal (“EAT”) ruled that the existence of an impending
internal appeal against a dismissal was not in itself sufficient to justify a
finding that it was not reasonably practicable to present a complaint to a
tribunal within the time limit. However, the EAT, in its judgment on Bodha ,
also stated as follows: 

 “There may be cases where the
special facts (additional to the bare fact that there is an internal appeal
pending) may persuade an industrial tribunal, as a question of fact, that it
was not reasonably practicable to complain to the industrial tribunal within
the time limit. But we do not think that the mere fact of a pending internal
appeal, by itself, is sufficient to justify a finding of fact that it was not
“reasonably practicable” to present a complaint to the industrial tribunal.” 

 19. 
 The EAT in the case of Inschape Retail Limited v
Mr A Shelton UKEAT/0142/19/JOJ considered this section of the Bodha 
judgment and found (at paragraphs 29 to 34 of its judgment on Inschape ) that
it was relevant to ask the following questions: 

 “It follows that an essential
question for the ET [Employment Tribunal] to consider in a case of this kind is
whether and to what extent the Claimant ought to have made enquiries into how
and within what period he should exercise his right to claim unfair dismissal.
As a matter of practical common sense an applicant who knows of his right to
claim unfair dismissal can generally be expected to seek information or advice
about the enforcement of those rights; see Porter v Bandridge Limited 
[1978] ICR 943 at 954 and Trevelyans (Birmingham) Limited v Norton 
[1991] ICR 65 at 68. 

 In these circumstances a mistaken
belief that an unfair dismissal claim need not be brought until after an
internal appeal procedure has been exhausted cannot of itself render it not
reasonably practicable to commence proceedings. It will depend what enquiries
the Claimant ought to have made and what knowledge he ought to have acquired. … 

 Whether it is reasonable for a
claimant to make enquiries and to what extent will be case specific. Claimants
in ETs vary enormously. On the one hand there are claimants with a good
education and command of English and ready access to the Internet and sources
of advice. It will generally be reasonably practicable for them to find out
about the enforcement of their rights, not least by using the Internet. It is
not difficult for an educated person to find out from official websites that
there is a strict time limit for bringing a complaint of unfair dismissal. 

 On the other hand, there are many
claimants with very limited education and English, health difficulties and
disabilities, and virtually no access to the Internet and sources of advice. It
may be much more difficult for them to obtain advice. 

 In this case the EJ [Employment
Judge] has accepted that the Claimant did not obtain advice and was labouring
under the misapprehension that he had to exhaust his internal appeal before
beginning proceedings. He has made no findings as to why the Claimant was
labouring under this misapprehension beyond saying that it was an
understandable view. 

 However, this depends on whether
it was reasonable to expect the Claimant to take steps to find out about the
enforcement of his rights. Was it reasonable to expect him to look at the
Internet, seek advice from sources available to him or even take legal advice
as he said he had? Once granted that the EJ accepted the Claimant’s evidence
that he had not, as he told the Respondent, taken any legal advice, it was
essential to address this issue before reaching a conclusion that it was not
reasonably practicable to commence proceedings in time.” 

 Conclusion 

 20. The Claimant asserts it would be just and equitable for the deadline for
bringing his claim to be extended on the basis he did not know he should bring
his claim alongside issuing an internal grievance and that he acted reasonably
and without delay. However, the relevant consideration is not whether it is
“just and equitable” to extend the deadline, but whether it was “reasonably
practicable” for the claim to be brought within the deadline. 

 21. While I am not bound to follow UK case law, and I note that it relates
to employment rather than discrimination claims, I nevertheless find the guidance
given by the EAT in the cases referred to above to be helpful in the current
case. Accordingly, the fact that the Claimant did not know he had to issue the
claim alongside bringing an internal grievance is not the relevant
consideration when determining whether it was reasonably practicable to bring a
claim. Instead, I should ask the question “ whether and to what extent the Claimant ought to
have made enquiries into how and within what period he should exercise his
right to claim”. 
The answer to this question will be fact specific depending on the particular
claimant. 

 22. In this
case, the Claimant is a professional, with the job title Fund Accountant and
Finance Officer, working for a well known bank. He has not made any assertions
that he does not have ready access to the internet and could not easily have accessed
the relevant information or advice from JACS or CAJ. I further note that the burden
of proof lies with the Claimant in this regard. 

 23. Even if the
Claimant did not know he had to bring his claim within 8 weeks of the date of
the incident about which he was complaining and he thought that it was
reasonable to wait for an internal grievance to be dealt with first, this does
not prevent it from being “reasonably practicable” to bring a claim. I find
that it would have been reasonable for the Claimant to have taken steps to find
out about the enforcement of his rights and, had he done so (for example, by performing
a simple internet search), the relevant information would have been freely
available to him. He has not satisfied me that he did this. 

 24. Consequently,
I do not have jurisdiction to extend the deadline within which the Claimant’s
claim can be presented and it remains rejected. 

 Mrs H
Westmacott, Deputy Chairman Sent to the Claimant on: 11 February 2026 

 Page Last Updated: 27 Mar 2026
